title: The Case Against Global Energy Bailouts
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition) 28 March 2026
content:
When global crises have struck in recent years, politicians in the rich world have been quick to turn to massive energy bailouts. During the pandemic and the Ukraine conflict, governments opened their chequebooks to heavily cushion the economic damage inflicted on households and businesses. Now, amid the Iran war, countries from Spain to Japan are actively cutting taxes, capping prices, and subsidizing energy bills. Even in energy-secure America, politicians have loudly called for windfall taxes on oil companies to fund direct handouts to consumers. However, such heavy-handed interventions often do more harm than good by artificially distorting critical market signals.

It is arithmetically impossible for every government to completely shield its citizens from a severe global energy shortage. With the Strait of Hormuz closed and major facilities damaged, the world's supply of liquefied natural gas has plummeted significantly. High prices naturally tell consumers to economize on energy while simultaneously encouraging producers to quickly find and sell more resources. By using public funds to artificially suppress prices, governments force the agonizing adjustment onto poorer nations that cannot afford subsidies. Consequently, regions like South Asia suffer crippling blackouts and deepening poverty while wealthier nations monopolize the remaining energy supplies.

Borrowing more money to aggressively subsidize energy consumption will rapidly eat up a country's scarce fiscal space. Unlike previous crises when interest rates were near zero, the current cost of debt is staggeringly high. By carelessly stimulating the economy, these massive bailouts make it much harder for central banks to effectively control soaring inflation. Instead of broad subsidies, governments should temporarily increase targeted cash payments strictly to the most vulnerable households. Ultimately, economies must be permanently weaned off insecure energy sources, a transition that cannot occur if governments endlessly subsidize fossil fuels.
